EPA141A · Model-Based Decision Making

Set-up and orientation

Understand the course structure, meet the models, and get your environment running from scratch.

Jazmin Zatarain Salazar

🏛 Delft University of Technology 🌊 Lake Problem (Labs) 🌍 JUSTICE climate–economy model (Assignments) 🐍 Python 3.10 – 3.14
Course
Labs & assignments overview
Models
The Lake Problem The JUSTICE Model
Labs
Lab overview (L1 – L6)
Assignments
Assignment overview How assignments connect Key packages
Setup
Install Python & VS Code 0Prerequisites 1Get the repository 2Create a virtual env 3Install dependencies 4Install JUSTICE 5Register the kernel 6Launch JupyterLab 7Verify installation
Help
Troubleshooting

Labs & Assignments Overview

The course is structured around six labs and eight assignments. Labs use a simple lake model to let you focus on learning the methods. Assignments apply the full MBDM cycle to JUSTICE, a climate–economy model, giving you experience with a real-world integrated assessment model.

The Lake Problem

The shallow lake eutrophication problem is the classic teaching model used in Labs 1 – 6. It is small enough to run thousands of simulations in seconds, making it ideal for exploring methods before applying them to the larger JUSTICE model.

What the model represents

A municipality releases phosphorus into a shallow lake year by year (from agriculture, sewage, etc.). Phosphorus builds up and, beyond a critical threshold, the lake flips into a eutrophic (algae-choked) state that is very hard to reverse. The question is: how much phosphorus can we safely release each year while maximising economic benefit?

Model structure

The lake model comes in two flavours used in different labs:

Intertemporal version (Labs 1 – 5) — the policy is a fixed schedule of 100 annual release rates decided in advance. This gives a 100-dimensional lever space.

DPS (Direct Policy Search) version (Lab 6) — the policy is a closed-loop rule: the release rate adapts to the current phosphorus level via five radial basis functions. This reduces the lever space to 5 dimensions and produces more realistic adaptive policies.

Uncertain parameters

b
phosphorus removal rate
q
recycling nonlinearity exponent
mean
mean natural inflow
stdev
variability of natural inflow
delta
discount rate (time preference)

Outcomes

OutcomeWhat it measuresGoal
max_PPeak phosphorus concentrationMinimise
utilityDiscounted sum of utility from releasesMaximise
inertiaFraction of years with small year-to-year changeMaximise
reliabilityFraction of years the lake stays below the critical thresholdMaximise
✅
The lake model is already included in the ema_workbench package — no separate installation is needed. The files labs/lakemodel_function.py and labs/dps_lake_model.py are thin wrappers that make it compatible with the EMA Workbench API.

The JUSTICE Model

JUSTICE (Just Climate Economy model) is a multi-regional Integrated Assessment Model (IAM) used in Assignments 1 – 8. It couples a neoclassical economy across 57 world regions with a physical climate model and a set of distributive justice welfare functions, allowing you to explore how different climate policies perform under deep uncertainty about both the physical climate and contested normative values.

Each world region produces output, emits CO₂, and suffers economic damages from warming. You control how aggressively each region cuts emissions (the emission control rate). The model returns how warm the planet gets, how costly the damages are, and how good or bad the outcome is according to different concepts of welfare. In Assignments 1–3 the policy is fixed; in Assignments 4–8 it is optimised using an evolutionary algorithm.

Repository layout

▶ Show / hide JUSTICE-main file tree 
JUSTICE-main/
├── justice/                  # Core model package
│   ├── model.py              # JUSTICE class — main entry point
│   ├── economy/
│   │   └── neoclassical.py   # GDP growth, capital, consumption
│   ├── climate/
│   │   ├── coupled_fair.py   # FaIR climate integration (emissions → temperature)
│   │   └── temperature_downscaler.py  # Global → regional temperatures
│   ├── damage/
│   │   └── kalkuhl.py        # Kalkuhl damage function (temperature → GDP loss)
│   ├── emissions/
│   │   └── emission.py       # Economic output → CO₂ emissions
│   ├── abatement/
│   │   └── abatement_enerdata.py  # Marginal abatement cost curves
│   ├── welfare/
│   │   └── social_welfare_function.py  # 8 justice welfare functions
│   ├── objectives/
│   │   └── objective_functions.py  # years_above_threshold, Gini, costs
│   └── util/
│       ├── EMA_model_wrapper.py    # EMA Workbench interface + RBF policy
│       ├── enumerations.py         # WelfareFunction, Scenario, etc.
│       ├── data_loader.py          # Loads HDF5 input datasets
│       └── output_data_processor.py  # Structures model outputs
├── solvers/
│   ├── moea/                 # Borg MOEA wrapper + Platypus adapter
│   ├── emodps/
│   │   └── rbf.py            # Radial Basis Function policy representation
│   └── convergence/          # Hypervolume, Pareto metrics
├── config/
│   ├── JUSTICE_config.yaml   # Master config (defaults, lever bounds)
│   └── default_parameters.py # Python defaults for each component
├── analysis/
│   └── hpc_run.py            # HPC cluster entry point
├── docs/diagrams/            # Architecture diagrams and logos
├── JUSTICE_example.py        # Standalone runnable example
└── run_optimization.py       # Full MOEA optimisation orchestration

Model components

ComponentClassWhat it does
Economy NeoclassicalEconomyModel
economy/neoclassical.py		 Cobb-Douglas production across 57 RICE-50 regions. Tracks capital accumulation, labour, consumption, and savings. Damage costs are subtracted from consumption each timestep.
Climate CoupledFAIR
climate/coupled_fair.py		 Wraps FaIR v2.1.3 to convert regional CO₂ emissions into global mean temperature. Supports 1001 calibrated ensemble members, each representing a different Equilibrium Climate Sensitivity (ECS).
Damage DamageKalkuhl
damage/kalkuhl.py		 Kalkuhl et al. damage function: temperature change relative to a baseline reduces regional GDP via empirically estimated coefficients. Includes lagged temperature effects.
Abatement AbatementEnerdata
abatement/abatement_enerdata.py		 Marginal Abatement Cost (MAC) curves from Enerdata data. Transitions from near-term empirical estimates through IPCC SR1.5 mid-century costs to a long-run backstop technology price.
Welfare SocialWelfareFunction
welfare/social_welfare_function.py		 Implements eight distributive justice principles: Utilitarian, Prioritarian, Sufficientarian, Egalitarian, Limitarian variants, and Consumption Corridor variants. In Assignments 1–3 you use the Utilitarian function.
Policy RBF / ECR matrix
solvers/emodps/rbf.py		 The emission control rate (ECR) is a matrix of shape (57 regions × timesteps). In Assignments 1–3 it is set to a fixed plateau value. In Assignments 4–8 it is an adaptive closed-loop policy encoded as Radial Basis Functions.

How the model runs

All assignment notebooks interact with JUSTICE through a thin wrapper function that matches the EMA Workbench function-model interface:

from justice.model import JUSTICE
from justice.util.enumerations import WelfareFunction

JUSTICE.hard_reset()                    # clear singleton state between runs
model = JUSTICE(
    start_year=2015, end_year=2300, timestep=1,
    scenario=2,                         # SSP2-RCP4.5
    climate_ensembles=1,                # single FaIR ensemble member
    social_welfare_function=WelfareFunction.UTILITARIAN,
)
model.run(emission_control_rate=ecr, endogenous_savings_rate=True)
datasets = model.evaluate()             # returns dict of output arrays
ℹ️
A single run (2015–2300 at 1-year timestep) takes roughly 1–3 seconds. The 100-scenario ensembles in Assignments 1–3 take 5–15 minutes sequentially.

Deep uncertain parameters (Assignments 1–3)

ρ (rho)
pure rate of social time preference
range [0.001, 0.030] — normative
η (eta)
elasticity of marginal utility
range [0.5, 1.5] — normative
δ (delta)
damage function scale factor
range [0.5, 2.0] — normative
ecs_ensemble
FaIR ensemble member index
range [1, 1001] — physical

Model outcomes

OutcomeWhat it measuresType
welfareAggregate discounted social welfare across all regions and yearsScalar
years_above_temperature_thresholdNumber of simulated years global mean temperature exceeds 2 °C above pre-industrialScalar
welfare_loss_damageWelfare lost due to climate damagesScalar
welfare_loss_abatementWelfare lost due to emission reduction costsScalar
temperature_trajectoryGlobal mean temperature time series 2015–2300Array (286 values)
⚠️
JUSTICE must be run from the JUSTICE-main/ directory — it resolves data files using relative paths. The setup cell at the top of every assignment notebook calls os.chdir() automatically. Always run the setup cell before any other cell.

Lab Overview

The six labs use the Lake Problem to teach each MBDM method in isolation before you apply it to JUSTICE in the assignments. Labs are shorter and more guided; each lab introduces one method that the corresponding assignment then applies independently.

# Title What you learn Key methods
L1 Exploratory Modelling Connect a model to EMA Workbench. Run open exploration across uncertain parameters and multiple policies. Visualise outcome distributions and pairwise trade-offs. EMA WorkbenchMonte CarloPairs scatter
L2 Sensitivity Analysis Quantify which uncertain inputs most strongly drive output variance. Compare three methods: OLS regression, Sobol indices, and Extra-Trees feature scoring. Sobol S₁/SₜSALibExtra-TreesLHS
L3 Scenario Discovery Given a fixed policy, find the combinations of uncertain conditions that cause it to fail. Interpret coverage, density, and the peeling trajectory of PRIM boxes. PRIMDimensional stackingScenario boxes
L4 MOEA Setup & Optimisation Define a many-objective problem and use ε-NSGA-II to search a 100-dimensional lever space. Explore how epsilon parameters control Pareto-front granularity. ε-NSGA-IIPareto dominanceε-progress
L5 Pareto Analysis & Convergence Load archived Pareto sets from multiple MOEA seeds. Compute six convergence metrics and build a cross-seed reference set using epsilon-nondominated sorting. HypervolumeGDε-indicatorSeed analysis
L6 MORDM Complete the full Multi-Objective Robust Decision Making cycle on the DPS lake model: optimise, re-evaluate under 1,000 scenarios, compute robustness metrics, and discover failure conditions. Signal-to-noiseMax regretDPS / RBFPRIM
ℹ️
Labs are located in the labs/ folder. They use the same EPA141A (JUSTICE) kernel as the assignments — all required packages (EMA Workbench, SALib, etc.) are already installed in the shared virtual environment.

Assignment Overview

The eight assignments form a single end-to-end MBDM workflow applied to the JUSTICE climate–economy model. Each assignment builds on the outputs of the previous ones. Start from A1 and work in order.

# Title What you do Key methods
A1 Exploratory Modeling Run JUSTICE across an ensemble of scenarios and characterise how uncertainty propagates through the model outcomes. LHSEMA WorkbenchEnsemble
A2 Sensitivity Analysis Identify which uncertain inputs matter most for each outcome and compare the results across sensitivity methods. Sobol S₁/SₜSALibExtra-Trees
A3 Scenario Discovery Find the conditions under which a given policy fails and describe the vulnerability space. PRIMScenario discovery
A4 Problem Formulation Define the decision problem — objectives, levers, uncertainties, and relationships — and choose a policy representation. XLRMRBFEMODPS
A5 MOEA Local Run Search the policy space for trade-off solutions using a many-objective evolutionary algorithm and inspect the Pareto front. Borg MOEAPareto front
A6 MOEA Convergence Assess whether the optimisation has converged and consolidate results across seeds into a reference set. ε-dominanceHypervolumeGD
A7 Pareto Visualisation Explore and communicate the trade-offs in the reference set and map the policy choices across world regions. Parallel coordsGeoPandasRICE-50
A8 Robustness Analysis Stress-test the Pareto policies across a broad range of scenarios and identify which policy is most robust. SatisficingRegretFAIR ensemble

How Assignments Connect

The assignments form a linear pipeline. Later assignments load outputs saved by earlier ones.

A1 → ensemble runs (saved to results/)
A2 → reads A1 ensemble → sensitivity indices
A3 → reads A1 ensemble → scenario boxes
A4 → defines objectives & policy structure → config_student.json
A5 → runs MOEA → Pareto CSVs in results/
A6 → merges A5 seeds → reference_set.csv
A7 → reads A6 reference set → plots & maps
A8 → reads A6 reference set → robustness metrics

Key Packages

The assignments rely on five groups of packages. All are installed automatically in Step 3; this table is a quick reference for when you want to read further.

Package Role in the assignments Documentation
ema-workbench Exploratory modelling & analysis workbench — built around the XLRM framework; connects uncertainties, levers, and outcomes into reproducible experiment ensembles emaworkbench.readthedocs.io
SALib Sensitivity analysis library salib.readthedocs.io
platypus-opt Many-objective evolutionary optimisation algorithms platypus.readthedocs.io
geopandas Geospatial data and mapping geopandas.org/docs
pandas · numpy · matplotlib · seaborn Data wrangling, numerical computation, and visualisation pandas · numpy · matplotlib · seaborn

Install Python, VS Code & Git

If you are starting from a completely fresh computer, follow these steps in order. If you already have Python, VS Code, and Git installed, skip to Prerequisites.

▶
1 — Using the terminal
All setup commands are typed into a terminal. Here is everything you need to know before starting.

How to open a terminal

Press ⌘ Space, type Terminal, press Enter.
Or in VS Code: Terminal → New Terminal (opens directly in your project folder).

Essential commands

cd ~/courses/epa141a   # navigate to the repo folder
ls                     # list files — you should see JUSTICE-main/
cd ..                  # go up one level if needed

Paste commands with ⌘V and run them with Enter.

How to open a terminal

Press Win, type cmd or PowerShell, press Enter.
Or in VS Code: Terminal → New Terminal (opens directly in your project folder).

Essential commands

cd C:\Users\yourname\courses\epa141a   # navigate to the repo folder
dir                                    # list files — you should see JUSTICE-main/
cd ..                                  # go up one level if needed

Paste commands with Ctrl+V and run them with Enter. Use .venv\Scripts\ instead of .venv/bin/ in every command in this guide.

How to open a terminal

Press Ctrl+Alt+T, or find Terminal in your app launcher.
Or in VS Code: Terminal → New Terminal.

Essential commands

cd ~/courses/epa141a   # navigate to the repo folder
ls                     # list files — you should see JUSTICE-main/
cd ..                  # go up one level if needed

Paste commands with Ctrl+V and run them with Enter.

✅
Every command in the rest of this guide must be run from the repository root — the folder containing JUSTICE-main/. Opening VS Code's built-in terminal puts you there automatically.
🐍
2 — Install Python 3.12
Python 3.12 is recommended. Any version from 3.10 to 3.14 works.

Option A — Homebrew (recommended)

Open Terminal, paste these commands one at a time:

# 1. Install Homebrew (skip if already installed)
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"

# 2. Install Python 3.12
brew install python@3.12

# 3. Verify
python3 --version

Option B — Official installer

Download the macOS .pkg installer from python.org/downloads, run it, then verify in Terminal:

python3 --version
⚠️
Do not use the pre-installed /usr/bin/python3 — the macOS system Python is too old and cannot be updated.

Option A — Official installer (recommended)

  1. Download the Windows installer (.exe) from python.org/downloads.
  2. Run it. On the first screen, tick "Add python.exe to PATH" — this is critical.
  3. Click Install Now.
  4. Open Command Prompt and verify:
python --version

Option B — Microsoft Store

Search for Python 3.12 in the Microsoft Store and click Get. Then verify in Command Prompt:

python --version
⚠️
On Windows use python (not python3) and replace .venv/bin/ with .venv\Scripts\ in every command in this guide.

Ubuntu / Debian

sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3.12 python3.12-venv python3-pip -y
python3.12 --version

Fedora / RHEL

sudo dnf install python3.12 -y
python3.12 --version
ℹ️
If your distro's default python3 is older than 3.10, use python3.12 -m venv .venv in Step 2 of the setup guide instead of python3 -m venv .venv.
</>
3 — Install VS Code (recommended editor)
VS Code has first-class Jupyter notebook support. JupyterLab in the browser also works — pick one.
  1. Download VS Code from code.visualstudio.com and run the installer for your platform.
  2. Open VS Code, click the Extensions icon in the left sidebar (or press Ctrl+Shift+X / ⌘⇧X), and install:
    • Python (by Microsoft) — Python language support and virtual-env detection
    • Jupyter (by Microsoft) — run .ipynb notebooks inside VS Code
  3. Open the repository folder: File → Open Folder, select the epa141a/ folder.
  4. Open any .ipynb file. VS Code will prompt you to select a kernel — choose EPA141A (JUSTICE) after completing Step 5 below.
✅
All eight assignments and six labs are tested in VS Code. The setup cell in each notebook detects the VS Code environment automatically via __vsc_ipynb_file__.
⎇
4 — Install Git
Needed only if you want to clone the repository. Not needed if you download a ZIP.
# Option A: Homebrew
brew install git

# Option B: Xcode Command Line Tools (already includes git)
xcode-select --install
  1. Download the installer from git-scm.com/download/win.
  2. Run it, accepting all defaults. This installs Git Bash, which you can use instead of Command Prompt for the commands in this guide.
# Ubuntu / Debian
sudo apt install git -y

# Fedora
sudo dnf install git -y

Verify: git --version should print something like git version 2.x.x.

Prerequisites

You need the following software installed before starting. No Python packages are needed yet — the steps below install everything inside an isolated virtual environment.

Python
3.10 – 3.14
Check: python3 --version
pip
≥ 23
Bundled with Python 3.10+
Git
any recent
Check: git --version
Disk space
≈ 2 GB
Dependencies + FAIR climate data
ℹ️
macOS users: install Python via Homebrew (brew install python@3.12) or the official installer at python.org. The system Python is too old.
ℹ️
Windows users: use python instead of python3, and replace .venv/bin/ with .venv\Scripts\ in every command below.

Step 1 — Get the Repository

1
Download or clone the course repository
This gives you the assignments, labs, and all configuration files.

Option A — Download as ZIP (no Git required)

  1. Go to the course repository page on GitHub / BrightSpace.
  2. Click Code → Download ZIP.
  3. Unzip to a location without spaces or special characters in the path (e.g. ~/courses/epa141a).

Option B — Git clone

git clone https://github.com/Hippo-Delft-AI-Lab/epa141a.git
cd epa141a

Option A — Download as ZIP (no Git required)

  1. Go to the course repository page on GitHub / BrightSpace.
  2. Click Code → Download ZIP.
  3. Unzip to a location without spaces or special characters in the path (e.g. C:\courses\epa141a).

Option B — Git clone

git clone https://github.com/Hippo-Delft-AI-Lab/epa141a.git
cd epa141a
ℹ️
You can use Git Bash (installed with Git) or PowerShell to run these commands. Avoid paths with spaces.

Option A — Download as ZIP (no Git required)

  1. Go to the course repository page on GitHub / BrightSpace.
  2. Click Code → Download ZIP.
  3. Unzip to a location without spaces or special characters in the path (e.g. ~/courses/epa141a).

Option B — Git clone

git clone https://github.com/Hippo-Delft-AI-Lab/epa141a.git
cd epa141a
⚠️
All subsequent commands must be run from the repository root — the folder that contains assignments_ema/ and requirements.txt.

Also clone the JUSTICE model

JUSTICE is maintained in a separate repository and must be cloned into a folder called JUSTICE-main/ inside the course repo root. From the repo root:

git clone https://github.com/pollockDeVis/JUSTICE.git JUSTICE-main
ℹ️
The folder must be named JUSTICE-main/ — the assignment notebooks and the install command in Step 4 both expect this exact name.

What's inside

  • JUSTICE-main/ — the JUSTICE climate–economy model (cloned separately above)
  • assignments_ema/ — student assignment notebooks (A1 – A8)
  • model_answers_ema/ — model answer notebooks
  • labs/ — lab notebooks (L1 – L6) using the Lake Problem
  • config/ — optimisation configuration files
  • requirements.txt — package list
  • requirements-lock.txt — exact pinned versions for reproducibility

Step 2 — Create a Virtual Environment

2
Create .venv inside the repo root
A virtual environment keeps all course packages isolated from your system Python.
python3 -m venv .venv
python -m venv .venv
✅
You should now have a .venv/ folder in the repo root. You never need to activate it manually — all commands below call the venv Python directly.

Step 3 — Install Dependencies

3
Install all packages from the lock file
The lock file pins exact versions that are known to work together. This takes 3–8 minutes on a first run.
.venv/bin/pip install -r requirements-lock.txt
.venv\Scripts\pip install -r requirements-lock.txt
ℹ️
Key version pin: fair == 2.1.3 — JUSTICE was developed and tested against this exact FAIR version. Do not upgrade it.

Step 4 — Install JUSTICE

4
Install JUSTICE in editable mode
Make the JUSTICE source you cloned in Step 1 importable. The --no-deps flag prevents JUSTICE from overwriting the pinned versions installed in Step 3.
.venv/bin/pip install -e JUSTICE-main --no-deps
.venv\Scripts\pip install -e JUSTICE-main --no-deps

The -e flag installs in editable mode — any changes to the JUSTICE source are immediately reflected without reinstalling. --no-deps prevents JUSTICE from overwriting the carefully pinned versions from Step 3.

ℹ️
After installation the packages justice, solvers, and analysis are importable from any notebook running the EPA141A kernel.

Step 5 — Register the Jupyter Kernel

5
Make the virtual environment available in JupyterLab
This registers a kernel named EPA141A (JUSTICE) that notebooks can select.
.venv/bin/python -m ipykernel install --user \
    --name epa141a \
    --display-name "EPA141A (JUSTICE)"
.venv\Scripts\python -m ipykernel install --user ^
    --name epa141a ^
    --display-name "EPA141A (JUSTICE)"

You only need to run this once. The kernel persists across JupyterLab sessions.

Step 6 — Launch JupyterLab

6
Start the notebook server
Always launch from the repo root so relative paths resolve correctly.
.venv/bin/jupyter lab
.venv\Scripts\jupyter lab

JupyterLab opens in your browser. Navigate to assignments_ema/ and open assignment_01_exploratory_modeling.ipynb. In the top-right kernel picker, select EPA141A (JUSTICE).

💡
VS Code users: open the repo folder in VS Code, open any .ipynb file, click the kernel picker in the top right, and choose EPA141A (JUSTICE). All assignments support VS Code natively — the setup cell detects __vsc_ipynb_file__ automatically.

Step 7 — Verify the Installation

7
Run the verification cell in Assignment 1
This confirms that JUSTICE, EMA Workbench, and all dependencies are correctly installed.

Open assignments_ema/assignment_01_exploratory_modeling.ipynb and run the first code cell (Step 0 — Verify your installation). You should see output like:

  ✓  justice
  ✓  numpy
  ✓  pandas
  ✓  matplotlib
  ✓  ema_workbench
  ✓  scipy
  ✓  seaborn

Python 3.12.x

✅ Success looks like

  • Every package shows ✓ — no ✗
  • No ModuleNotFoundError for justice, ema_workbench, or fair
  • No red error tracebacks in subsequent cells
⚠️
If you see RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in log from the FAIR climate model — this is harmless. FAIR catches it internally and the results are still valid.

Troubleshooting

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'justice'

Either JUSTICE was not cloned or not installed. First confirm that a JUSTICE-main/ folder exists in the repo root (if not, re-run the clone command from Step 1). Then re-run Step 4 and confirm the EPA141A (JUSTICE) kernel is selected in JupyterLab.

Kernel not found / kernel keeps dying

Re-run Step 5 to re-register the kernel. If the kernel dies immediately, check available RAM — JUSTICE loads FAIR climate data on startup and requires at least 2 GB free.

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'ema_workbench'

You are running the wrong Python. Confirm with:

.venv/bin/python -c "import ema_workbench; print(ema_workbench.__version__)"

Expected output: 3.0.x. If the command fails, re-run Step 3.

FileNotFoundError for JUSTICE data files

JUSTICE must be run from the JUSTICE-main/ directory — it resolves data paths relatively. The setup cell in every assignment handles this with os.chdir(_JUSTICE_ROOT). Make sure you run the setup cell before any other cell.

RecursionError in perform_experiments (Python 3.14 + tqdm)

A known incompatibility between Python 3.14 and tqdm ≥ 4.67 in Jupyter notebooks. The assignment notebooks include a one-line fix (tqdm.tqdm = tqdm.std.tqdm) in the experiments cell. Make sure you have the latest version of the notebooks from the repository and restart the kernel before re-running.

reference_set_utilitarian.csv not found (A7, A8)

Run Assignment 6 first and execute all cells in order. The file is saved to model_answers_ema/results/.

RuntimeWarning from FAIR (invalid value in log)

Harmless — see Step 7. Ignore it.

Slow first run in A8

A8 runs 28 policies × 50 FAIR scenarios sequentially in the notebook (≈ 20–40 min, one time only). Results are cached to results/reeval_utilitarian_28p_50s.npy and loaded instantly on subsequent runs. To run faster with multiprocessing, use the companion script from a terminal:

.venv/bin/python model_answers_ema/run_reeval.py --n_scenarios 50

Platform-specific: Windows path separators

Replace all .venv/bin/ prefixes with .venv\Scripts\ on Windows. All other commands are identical.

EPA141A Model-Based Decision Making · Delft University of Technology · Jazmin Zatarain Salazar
